item.description: But without “gold standard” constraints, the pact could allow Riyadh to eventually develop a warhead.

item.title: Capitalism’s status hierarchy is being upturned
item.description: One of the modern world’s most powerful forces is among its least understood

item.title: The Race to Contain the Fastest-Ever Ebola Outbreak
item.description: According to the WHO, the epidemic is “outpacing” response efforts.

item.title: India’s Cockroach Movement Delivers a Blow to the BJP
item.description: The protests aren’t likely to turn into mass mobilization, but they are politically significant.

item.title: The tariffs that just won’t die

item.description: In an era of change, Andy Burnham promises to rewind the clock

item.title: Does SpaceX’s plan for a data centre in space add up?
item.description: It’s less crazy than you think

item.title: Trump’s Epic Fury Can’t Resolve His Iran Quagmire
item.description: The clock is ticking for global oil markets, and Iran has the time.

item.title: India’s Gen-Z protesters demand a proper education
item.description: Delhi’s biggest protests in years have been met by tear-gas and repression

item.title: The World Cup and the G2 Reality
item.description: The international sporting event showcased American strengths. It's a reminder that both the U.S. and China should stop betting on each other’s decline.

item.title: Yemen’s Unsteady Stalemate
item.description: As violence rises, both sides seek to increase their leverage without returning to war.

item.title: India’s High-Stakes Gamble in the SCO
item.description: In an era of diminishing multilateralism, can the SCO actually bring results?

item.title: What the New UK Chancellor Means for the UK Economy
item.description: By appointing John Healey as chancellor of the exchequer, British Prime Minister Andy Burnham has taken a calculated risk. If Healey can leverage his insider knowledge of the Treasury, pursue a bold, pro-growth agenda, and manage his defense-spending instincts, he may be able to provide the structural shake-up the UK economy needs.

item.title: Will Trump’s War With Canada Become Global?
item.description: For the first time, a great power has moved to price transboundary climate-driven harms–massive wildfires–and extract compensation unilaterally. What was previously a development, insurance, or emissions-accounting issue is now a security and trade dispute between two G7 economies, setting a precedent that others may try to follow.

item.title: Why a Tibetan Protester Set Himself on Fire Outside the United Nations
item.description: Loga Rangzen’s self-immolation was intended to force Tibet back onto the international agenda. Instead, it exposed the challenges Tibetans face amid China's growing global influence.

item.title: Keep an Eye on China’s AI Governance Offensive
item.description: Beijing’s intensifying efforts to shape international norms on AI are aimed at catching up on the technology and exporting China’s illiberal values.

item.title: America Is Closing the Door on Opportunity—and Talent
item.description: Viewed in isolation, the Trump administration's new, more restrictive student-visa policy may seem modest. But as part of a broader retreat from openness, it signals that the country once known for offering ambitious outsiders a chance to transform their lives is less willing to do so.

item.title: Electric aviation is taking off
item.description: Hybrids, rather than fully electric planes, are likely to triumph

item.title: Large Language Models for Small Language Communities
item.description: Many developing economies cannot access large language models, most of which have been trained on datasets in English. To build scalable and inclusive LLMs for countries with minority languages, policymakers' best bet is to tap mobile network operators, with their armies of developers and data-processing capabilities.

item.title: The Epstein Class Was Made Offshore
item.description: The impunity of Jeffrey Epstein and his associates is the predictable upshot of unbridled wealth concentration, facilitated by the offshore financial system. Extreme economic inequality inevitably creates political inequality, which can leads to the exemption of the privileged few from even the most basic moral norms.

item.title: Indian Army’s Integrated Battle Groups Are Crystallizing ‘Cold Start’ Doctrine
item.description: Given the uncertainty in the region's escalation ladder, IBGs may be the answer if the need for a quick ground retort were to arise.

item.title: China Pitches Its AI Leadership to the World
item.description: A new organization includes five Southeast Asian members.

item.title: When China Gets Its Own Mythos
item.description: Preparing for an AI cyber crisis.

item.title: The Iran Cease-Fire Was Doomed to Fail
item.description: Why transactionalism won’t deliver peace in the Middle East.

item.title: Trump’s China Threats Don’t Match His China Policy
item.description: The president’s allegations about election interference are the latest case in point.

item.title: Why a Hunger Strike Is Rallying India’s Youth
item.description: Sonam Wangchuk’s fast in solidarity with Cockroach movement protests joins a long tradition.

item.title: The Oil Market Is Much More Vulnerable Than Trump Believes
item.description: Depleted reserves and changed circumstances make price spikes more likely this time around.

item.title: Who Is Trump’s Next Tariff Target?
item.description: Trump’s latest protectionist measures will likely exacerbate trade tensions and reignite legal battles.

item.title: The Iran war could consume the Trump presidency
item.description: Donald Trump may hate to hear it, but Barack Obama was right about avoiding “stupid shit”

item.description: Demand for open-weight models is soaring

item.description: An arms show in Paris shows how global backlash is forcing the country’s booming defense industry to adapt.

item.title: Amnesty: Deadly Pakistan Air Strikes on Kabul Drug Rehab Center a Possible War Crime
item.description: The human rights organization’s new report found “no evidence of Pakistan’s claims” that the site hit on March 16 was a military target.

item.title: The Iran Expert Trump Fired
item.description: Nate Swanson on the U.S. president’s unique top-down style—and his fondness for lobbying.

item.title: Where should you move for higher pay?
item.description: Some cities merely attract the well remunerated. Others will lift your wages

item.description: Fewer than ever believe central bankers will bring it back to target

item.description: Fewer than ever believe central bankers will bring it back to target

item.title: When a president stops pretending that voters count, disaster beckons
item.description: Tiny Nicaragua offers a cautionary tale for democrats everywhere

item.description: With China’s latest launch of frontier model Kimi K3, the U.S. must also focus on open source AI.

item.title: Dinosaurs Roam the Aspen Security Forum
item.description: This year’s conference could have been held in 2003, 2008, or 2011.

item.title: The Limits – and Dangers – of the China-Russia Partnership
item.description: Insights from Mathieu Boulègue.

item.title: Security Issues Are Overshadowing Kashmir’s Worsening Climate Crisis
item.description: Kashmir's mountains are going to keep getting hotter whether anyone notices or not.

item.title: A New Tool for Combating Human Trafficking
item.description: While human trafficking is mostly framed as a macro phenomenon, this does not fully explain how these criminal networks operate. Recognizing that trafficking corridors link specific source and destination localities offers a stronger basis for policy than a purely national approach or isolated interventions.

item.title: Beyond a ‘Non-Red’ Supply Chain, Taiwan Should Export a Drone Ecosystem
item.description: To build a real “drone shield,” Taiwan cannot merely export hardware or components.

item.title: There’s a Fix for Israel’s Image Crisis
item.description: A reluctant opposition needs a radically new pathway on Palestine.

item.title: How South Korea’s Africa Pledges Are Faring on the Ground
item.description: In western Uganda, a project funded by the Korea International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) serves as a test case for what South Korea can offer the continent.

item.title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly in Global Tax Negotiations
item.description: Ongoing negotiations at the United Nations offer the international community the best chance that it will have to ensure that global taxation authority is assigned fairly. With multinationals and the ultra-rich abusing the current system to minimize their tax payments, finalizing a new convention is an urgent priority.

item.title: China’s shadowy maritime militia is worryingly active
item.description: Big new formations north of Taiwan suggest officials are testing out its capabilities

item.title: The Case for Banning Anti-Democratic Parties
item.description: The history of the 20th century shows that democracies sometimes must pursue seemingly undemocratic measures against those whose pursuit of power is motivated by a desire to abolish democracy altogether. Arguments that such a response is self-defeating do not withstand scrutiny.

item.title: China’s Live-Fire Drill Near Okinotorishima Tests Japan’s Maritime Nerves
item.description: The exercise highlights growing China-Russia naval interoperability while exposing competing interpretations of maritime law.

item.title: South America is eradicating hunger
item.description: Thank central banks and savvy handouts

item.title: The Bayer Truth About Trump’s Farm and Food Policies
item.description: A new executive order on regenerative agriculture might seem to suggest that US President Donald Trump’s administration wants to help American farmers. But a closer look at the order and other recent developments reveals a familiar pattern: an unhappy public gets reassuring words, while corporate interests receive tangible support.

item.title: Don’t Overestimate America and China’s Importance
item.description: Some commentators on international affairs seem to think that “the West” and the BRICS+ do not really matter; only the United States and China do. Yet three factors complicate this simplistic framing, suggesting that even superpowers' influence has limits, and that smaller powers can sometimes punch above their weight.

item.title: Modi’s Great Anglo Paradox
item.description: Cultural decolonization advances alongside unprecedented Western partnerships.

item.title: The Western Myth of Russian Greatness
item.description: There is a long history of foreigners idealizing Russia and exaggerating its power.

item.title: Transnational Repression, Organized Crime, and Conflict in Southeast Asia
item.description: A conversation with Phil Robertson, a Bangkok-based human rights advocate.

item.title: Does Myanmar Still Have One Government to Recognize?
item.description: Much of the world still views Myanmar through a narrow diplomatic lens that cannot encompass the full political complexity of its conflict.

item.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers Convene Meeting Under the Shadow of Iran War, Regional Crises
item.description: The bloc's foreign ministers are set to call for "the restoration of the safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”

item.title: Trump Needs to Defy History to End the Iran War
item.description: U.S. presidents are virtually never able to end the stalemated wars they start.

item.title: Armenian Democracy Faces a Threat From Within
item.description: A predatory opposition, fueled by external support, is gaining influence within the state.

item.description: Despots hate cities and the mayors who run them.

item.description: The two sides traded accusations over the confrontation at Second Thomas Shoal, a long-standing focus of maritime tensions between Manila and Beijing.

item.description: The report comes amid growing U.S. efforts to overturn Cuba’s government.

item.description: The move could increase pressure on already-strained global energy markets.

item.description: Alessandro Ford, a Latin America contributor, explains why the rivalry runs deeper than the game

item.description: Michael Reid, our Spain correspondent, on why the football team embodies the best of the country

item.description: Joseph Aoun, who meets Mr Trump on July 21st, must quash Hizbullah, repair war damage and fix the economy

item.description: Hormuz is a sign of worse things to come.

item.description: Better skin, faster wound healing and reduced vision loss are on the cards

item.description: As Kazakhstan pushes forward to become a leading tech hub in the region, China continues to prove itself a crucial partner.

item.description: Global tech governance is taking an institutional turn as a bloc-based system continues to solidify.

item.description: The law forbids publicly damaging, removing, or defacing a Japanese flag in a manner likely to cause others “extreme discomfort or disgust.”

item.title: Suwayda’s Struggle
item.description: Syria’s Druze are trapped between Israeli protection and Damascus’s rule.

item.description: The Cockroach Janata Party is leading a youth-heavy protest movement that embodies the public anger with the BJP.

item.description: The World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization may prove to be China's most ambitious effort yet to shape the future deployment and governance of AI.

item.title: Don’t blame supermarkets for your expensive groceries
item.description: Saying greedy shops have caused rising food prices is populist slop

item.description: It risks inflaming ethnic tensions and undermining territorial claims

item.description: Journalism can help ensure that climate governance is guided by transparency, accountability, and justice.

item.title: UNCCD Executive Secretary Yasmine Fouad on Mongolia, Desertification, and COP17
item.description: COP17, which will be hosted by Mongolia in August, “comes at a pivotal moment for global action on land.”

item.title: Colombia’s Rightward Turn
item.description: There is a growing tension in Colombia between the popular demand for a strong welfare state and a political system that cannot deliver on that promise unless it breaks free from malign influences. Since the incoming administration is aligned with many of those interests, Colombians will continue to be disappointed.

item.title: A Cold War Adventure in Portugal
item.description: Just 52 years after escaping military dictatorship, Portugal is an EU and eurozone member and the world’s seventh-safest country. While Donald Trump's systematic destruction of the rules-based global order will not reverse this progress, nor will it do any good for Portugal, the US, or anyone else.

item.title: Europe Is Back in 1952
item.description: France and Germany again locked into military rivalry is bad news for the rest of Europe.

item.title: China’s Arctic Comeback
item.description: Many of China’s initial, ambitious Arctic objectives collided with political and logistical realities. Now Beijing is testing out an “Arctic 2.0” strategy.

item.description: Firing the reformist defence minister has plunged Volodymyr Zelensky’s government into crisis

item.description: The world’s great powers are engaging a new battleground in their search for critical minerals.

item.title: Australia’s Tuvalu Migration Deal Tests the Limits of Climate Mobility
item.description: The Falepili Union offers Tuvaluans “mobility with dignity.” But unless Australia also protects Tuvalu’s workforce, the landmark agreement could weaken the country years before rising seas make it uninhabitable.

item.title: Social-Media Age Bans Won’t Make Children Safer—But This Will
item.description: As the European Union considers how to protect and empower young people in a digital world, it must resist the urge to enact a ban on social media for young people. A more effective strategy would force tech companies to remove addictive features by holding them to product-liability standards—as with most other industries.

item.title: 5 Myths About the Five Eyes
item.description: There are persistent misunderstandings about what the intelligence partnership is, and what it actually does.

item.title: Why Europe’s China Anxiety Is About More Than China
item.description: Brussels no longer sees China only as an economic actor. It sees China as a challenge to the organization of global order itself.

item.description: A new bill aims to stop the United States from “funding abuse.”

item.title: Beyond the South China Sea: 4 Other Maritime Flashpoints in East Asia
item.description: These disputes could reshape the geopolitical order in East Asia – or even spark a war.

item.title: Britain’s New Prime Minister Must Stand Up to Trump
item.description: Keir Starmer allowed his country to be humiliated by Washington. Burnham should learn from his mistakes.

item.title: Flamingos Against Autocrats
item.description: How a Gen Z-led protest movement in Albania is calling out corruption and global oligarchy.

item.title: Who Will Sit Atop the Next World Order?
item.description: American hegemony has given way to a global order based on rivalry and competition among several major powers, where there is no longer a fixed, codified set of rules. Instead, there is only economic, technological, and military power—the likes of which China is amassing faster than many observers realize.

item.title: Ongoing Belt and Road Initiative Poses Strategic Risks in the Western Balkans
item.description: Infrastructure development, with a catch.

item.title: AI Will Supercharge Surveillance Capitalism
item.description: For too long, governments have stood by and allowed the creation of business models built on the commercial exploitation of users' personal data and that reward social and psychological manipulation. With the AI industry poised to replicate this model, policymakers must intervene before it is too late.

item.title: Is Thailand’s PM More Concerned About Sound Cannabis Policy or Political Optics?
item.description: Amid a rise in smuggling, Anutin Charnvirakul has recently come out against the policy he once championed: the decriminalization of marijuana.

item.description: Last week, the Special Advisory Council for Myanmar said that agreeing to the meeting was a sign of the ASEAN special envoy's "timidity and poor judgment."

item.description: Taslima Nasrin was driven out of Kolkata 19 years ago under pressure from Muslim fundamentalists. Now, Hindu nationalists are rolling out the red carpet for her.

item.description: Commemorating the 250th anniversary of the United States requires an honest appraisal of not only its founding principles but also its racist and imperialist legacy. A clear-eyed assessment shows why many people in the Global South remain cynical about the country’s ability to live up to its ideals.

item.description: Both sides are trying to sustain trade talks and practical cooperation, but the EU’s new security definition of China may complicate this progress.

item.description: Taiwan’s diplomatic space is shrinking along a vital Pacific energy route.

item.title: Boomers have the good life, but it could be longer

item.title: A Turning Point for African Industrialization
item.description: Building on the success of his mega-refinery in Lagos, Nigerian industrialist Aliko Dangote is in talks to construct a second one in collaboration with several East African countries. African leaders should learn from Dangote’s strategy of adding value to the continent’s natural resources and fostering cross-border cooperation.

item.title: What India’s Operationalization of Integrated Battle Groups Means for Pakistan
item.description: Since the inception of the IBGs, they have been framed as formations to launch offensive operations against Pakistan under the nuclear overhang. Now they are finally a reality.

item.description: Soldiers seem to be at particular risk of hypogonadism

item.title: Subsidies Do Not Explain China’s Competitiveness
item.description: Like every major economy, China uses industrial policy, and its subsidies have mattered. But subsidies are no longer the most convincing explanation for Chinese firms’ emergence as global leaders in industries that used to be the exclusive domain of advanced economies.

item.title: How to Isolate Tehran
item.description: Opening Hormuz requires marrying force with effective diplomacy.

item.title: Progress on the China-Kyrgyzstan-Uzbekistan Railway, Some Challenges Remain
item.description: Under discussion for 20 years, the CKU Railway is finally under construction with some hurdles surpassed and other challenges ahead.

item.description: The trilateral is more feasible – and more important – than ever before.

item.title: Putin Likes His Worst Option in Ukraine
item.description: Faced with a quagmire in eastern Ukraine and escalating Ukrainian attacks within Russia, Vladimir Putin must either seek a ceasefire or pursue some form of escalation. With all signs pointing to him embracing the latter alternative, the situation could worsen dramatically for everyone, but especially for him.

item.description: India and other Kremlin customers will benefit from an easing of restrictions.

item.title: The Subversion of the International Criminal Court
item.description: Already under US sanctions for his role in securing an arrest warrant for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, the chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court is now at risk of being removed altogether. If member states do not stand up for judicial independence now, they may never get another chance.

item.title: What Inequality Does to Electoral Democracy
item.description: Like many advanced democracies, France has long cycled between periods in which its politics were divided along traditional left-right redistributionist lines, and those in which culture war issues took center stage. If there is any lesson to be drawn from this history, it is that the cycle may be about to repeat.

item.title: Is India Planning an Exchange of Territory With Myanmar?
item.description: The territory that could be considered for exchange lies in Manipur’s Chandel district in India, contiguous to Myanmar’s Kabaw Valley in Sagaing Region.

item.description: At a time of growing regional turbulence, nations across the region are seeking to equip themselves with more and better armaments.

item.description: The Department of Foreign Affairs demanded the "immediate takedown” of the AI-generated video, in which the Philippines was depicted as a monkey.

item.description: The Pentagon is broken, but Congress can fix it.

item.title: Where Will Global Financial Fragmentation Lead?
